title: Routing Rules
description: Inspect the binding table, add pattern-based route rules, test pattern matching, set rule priorities, and verify that messages are routed to the correct workspace or agent.

steps.instruction: View the current binding table to understand how messages are routed by default.
steps.action: The terminal runs `openclaw routing table` which displays the default route entries in a table format: channel, pattern, target workspace, priority.
steps.expectedOutput: A table is printed with the default `* -> my-agent` catch-all rule at priority 0. The columns are `channel | pattern | target | priority`.

steps.instruction: Add a route rule that sends messages starting with `/code` to a `developer` workspace.
steps.action: The terminal runs `openclaw routing add --pattern "/code*" --target developer --channel telegram` which inserts a new entry into the binding table.
steps.expectedOutput: `Route added: /code* -> developer (telegram)`. `openclaw routing table` now shows the new rule alongside the default catch-all.

steps.instruction: Test the pattern match by sending `/code write a Python hello world` to the Telegram channel.
steps.action: The incoming message is evaluated against the binding table. The `/code` prefix matches the new rule, and the message is dispatched to the `developer` workspace instead of the default.
steps.expectedOutput: The Gateway log shows `Route matched: /code* -> developer`. The agent in the `developer` workspace responds with Python code.

steps.instruction: Set the priority of the `/code` rule to 10 so it takes precedence over other rules.
steps.action: The terminal runs `openclaw routing priority --pattern "/code*" --set 10`. The binding table is reordered so higher-priority rules are evaluated first.
steps.expectedOutput: `Priority updated: /code* -> 10`. The routing table now lists the `/code*` rule at priority 10 above any lower-priority rules.

steps.instruction: Verify routing by sending a regular message and a `/code` message, confirming each goes to the correct workspace.
steps.action: Two messages are sent: `Hello` (no prefix) and `/code explain closures`. The first matches the catch-all and goes to `my-agent`; the second matches the high-priority rule and goes to `developer`.
steps.expectedOutput: The Gateway log shows `Route matched: * -> my-agent` for `Hello` and `Route matched: /code* -> developer` for the second message. Both agents respond correctly.

completionMessage: Congratulations! Your routing table is configured with pattern-based rules and priorities. Messages are now intelligently dispatched to the correct workspace based on their content.
